Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned P340 (306PS AJ126) engine typically costs £4,500–£11,000 supply only. Fitting adds £900–£2,500 depending on workshop location. For newer models like the Velar, replacement costs can range from £5,000 to £8,000. Main dealer new engines can exceed £10,000 supply only.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ126 is a great engine, but its cooling system requires attention. Coolant warnings are extremely common and should never be ignored — they can lead to overheating, warped cylinder heads, or even full engine failure. The engine is also sensitive to oil sludge build-up, making frequent oil changes (every 8,000 miles) critical.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system failures (expansion tank cracks, thermostat housing leaks, water pump leaks), supercharger coupler wear (rattling and 'clonk' on shutdown), and timing chain tensioner wear (rattle on cold start). The cooling system is the most common and urgent issue.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Velar P340 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Land Rover scrap threshold: if your Velar is worth £20,000 and a recon engine is £7,000 (35%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £12,000 example, the same engine exceeds 50% — get a scrap valuation first. These are high-value vehicles that often justify replacement spend.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The P340 uses the 306PS (also known as the AJ126) — a 3.0-litre supercharged V6 producing 340 PS. It was fitted to the Velar L560 from 2017 to 2020. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the cooling system, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system components can be repaired individually — expansion tank, thermostat housing, water pump. However, ignoring coolant warnings can lead to catastrophic engine failure, requiring a full replacement. Address any coolant leaks immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the only engine generation this badge ever used — the 306PS (AJ126). See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. For newer models like the Velar, replacement costs can range from £5,000 to £8,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover Velar P340 engine typically takes 15–25 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The tightly packed engine bay and complex supercharger system add significant labour time compared to simpler engines.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Velar P340 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (Expansion Tank, Thermostat Housing, Water Pump)
problems.symptoms: Intermittent "Coolant Level Low" warning, sweet coolant smell, small drips or crusted coolant on hoses, overheating during idling, steam from under the hood.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — often age-related as plastic components become brittle.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; ignoring coolant warnings can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the coolant warning comes on, pull over and let the engine cool. Never open the tank while hot.

problems.recommendation: The 3.0L V6 uses a tightly engineered cooling system with numerous plastic components. The most common cause of coolant warnings is a cracked expansion tank. The thermostat housing is another well-known failure point. If you see any coolant warnings, have the system inspected immediately. Even a one-time warning nearly always means a leak is developing, and this engine can overheat quickly if coolant drops even slightly.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear
problems.symptoms: Diesel-like ticking and rattling from the engine, loud "clonk" from the top of the engine when turning off, rattling that sounds like "marbles in a tin can" — loudest on cold start, decreasing as the engine warms up.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if left for many thousands of miles, supercharger performance will degrade.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if you hear rattling or the "clonk".

problems.recommendation: The supercharger coupler/torsion isolator is a known wear item on the AJ126. The good news is that a worn coupler is normally nothing to worry about and is just annoying. However, if it is left for many thousands of miles, it will eventually wear so much that supercharger performance will degrade. If you hear the characteristic "clonk" when turning off the engine or a "marbles in a tin can" rattle on cold start, have the coupler inspected.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling timing chains — much more regular and higher pitched than a coupler rattle, worse with a cold engine. Can eventually lead to a stretched or skipped chain, causing pistons to hit valves and destroying the engine.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if the chain snaps, it's an interference engine — catastrophic failure requiring full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: at first sign of metallic rattle on cold start, book inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The AJ126 is nowhere near as bad for timing chain failure as the early 5.0-litre AJ133 V8, but timing chains can still be a problem. The number one enemy of these engines is oil sludge build-up. To prevent timing chain issues, change the engine oil far more often than the scheduled 16,000-mile interval — roughly every 8,000 miles. If you hear a regular, high-pitched rattle on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Velar P340 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Simple, cheap fix — do it immediately to avoid overheating
Well-known failure point — repair when symptoms appear
Preventative — catches fault before catastrophic failure
Interference engine — piston-valve contact means little is left to repair

buyingChecks.title: Buying This P340? Check These First
buyIf: * No coolant warnings or evidence of coolant leaks
* Full service history with oil changes every 8,000 miles or less (not the 16,000-mile interval)
* Supercharger coupler inspected and confirmed healthy
* No rattling or "clonk" from the supercharger area
avoidIf: * Coolant warning lights or evidence of leaks — cooling system issues are common
* Rattling from the engine — could be timing chain or supercharger coupler wear
* Poor service history — these engines are sensitive to oil sludge build-up
* Overheating or coolant loss — can cause warped cylinder heads or full engine failure

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The AJ126 is a great engine when properly maintained, but its cooling system requires attention and it is sensitive to oil sludge build-up. Coolant warnings are extremely common and should never be ignored. A £7,000 reconditioned engine on a Velar valued at £20,000 is 35% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£12,000 example, think carefully. The 60% Land Rover scrap threshold applies.

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover Velar P340 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Velar P340 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Coolant Level — Check for low coolant or visible leaks — the most common issue
* Coolant Warning — Any intermittent "Coolant Level Low" warnings?
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling on startup (timing chain or supercharger coupler wear)
* Supercharger Noise — Any "clonk" when turning off? "Marbles in a tin can" rattle?
* Service History — Oil changes every 8,000 miles (not the 16,000-mile interval)
* Engine Bay — Check for coolant residue or crusted coolant on hoses
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions failures and corrosion
